title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
LLM Fine-Tuning
Basics, Methods, and Practical Workflow
A beginner-friendly guide to adapting large language models for specific tasks, domains, and
behaviors.
Quick Summary
Core idea: Fine-tuning means continuing the training of a pre-trained LLM on a smaller,
task-specific dataset so it learns a desired style, domain, output format, or behavior.
Best for: repeated patterns, domain language, structured outputs, classification,
extraction, support replies, coding style, and internal process knowledge.
Not best for: frequently changing facts, private knowledge that must stay editable, or cases
where retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) can supply context more safely.
1. What is LLM fine-tuning?
A large language model is first pre-trained on huge amounts of general text. This gives it
broad

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
at is LLM fine-tuning?
A large language model is first pre-trained on huge amounts of general text. This gives it
broad language ability, reasoning patterns, and general knowledge of language structure.
Fine-tuning is a later training step where the model is shown a smaller, carefully prepared
dataset for a specific purpose.
KEY: Simple way to understand it: pre-training teaches the model language broadly.
 Fine-tuning teaches it how you want it to respond in a narrower situation.
For example, a general LLM can answer many types of questions. A fine-tuned customer-support
model can learn to answer in a company preferred tone, format, policy style, and escalation
pattern.
2. Why fine-tune an LLM?
Fine-tuning is useful when you need consistent behavior across many similar requests.
Instead

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
2. Why fine-tune an LLM?
Fine-tuning is useful when you need consistent behavior across many similar requests.
Instead of writing very long prompts every time, you train the model to naturally follow the
expected pattern.
- Improving output format consistency
- Teaching a specific tone or writing style
- Adapting the model to domain vocabulary
- Improving classification or extraction accuracy
- Making the model follow a company process
- Teaching coding conventions or response templates
- Reducing repeated prompt instructions
3. Fine-tuning vs prompting vs RAG
Prompting: gives instructions at request time. Use when you need quick behavior control
without training.
RAG: retrieves relevant documents and passes them into the prompt. Use when the answer
depends on changing, private, or

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
RAG: retrieves relevant documents and passes them into the prompt. Use when the answer
depends on changing, private, or document-based knowledge.
Fine-tuning: updates model behavior using examples. Use when you need repeatable behavior,
style, format, or domain adaptation.
KEY: Practical rule: use prompting first, add RAG when the model needs external knowledge,
LLM Fine-Tuning Basics | Page 1
and fine-tune when the same behavior must be learned and repeated reliably.
4. Main types of fine-tuning
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): train on input-output examples. Used to teach task behavior,
response style, or answer format.
Instruction fine-tuning: train on many instruction-response pairs. Used to make the model
follow user instructions better.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT): train a

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
response pairs. Used to make the model
follow user instructions better.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT): train a small number of added or selected parameters
instead of all weights. Common methods include LoRA and adapters.
Preference tuning / RLHF: use human or AI preference signals to rank better responses. Used
to improve helpfulness, safety, tone, and alignment.
5. The fine-tuning workflow
1. Define the target behavior clearly: decide the task, tone, format, domain, and success
 criteria.
2. Collect high-quality examples: each example should show the exact input and ideal
 output.
3. Clean the dataset: remove duplicates, incorrect answers, sensitive data, conflicting
 instructions, and noisy formatting.
4. Split the data: use separate training, validation, and test sets so

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
conflicting
 instructions, and noisy formatting.
4. Split the data: use separate training, validation, and test sets so performance can be
 checked on unseen examples.
5. Choose the base model and method based on cost, latency, quality, context length,
 deployment needs, and available compute.
6. Train carefully: tune hyperparameters such as learning rate, batch size, epochs, and
 sequence length.
7. Evaluate against a baseline: compare the fine-tuned model with the original model and a
 strong prompted version.
8. Deploy and monitor: track real-world failures, collect feedback, and improve the dataset
 over time.
6. Dataset basics
The dataset is the most important part of fine-tuning. A small, clean dataset with strong
examples usually beats a large, messy dataset.
Good examples: clear

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
of fine-tuning. A small, clean dataset with strong
examples usually beats a large, messy dataset.
Good examples: clear instructions, correct outputs, consistent formatting, realistic user
inputs, and edge cases.
Bad examples: wrong answers, vague outputs, duplicates, mixed styles, hidden assumptions,
and private data.
Size: the required size depends on the task. Some narrow formatting tasks may work with
hundreds of examples; broader behavior may need thousands or more.
Format: many systems use JSONL-style records with messages such as system, user, and
assistant, or prompt-completion pairs.
CODE: {"messages":[{"role":"system","content":"You are a helpful support
 assistant."},{"role":"user","content":"How do I reset my
 password?"},{"role":"assistant","content":"You can reset your

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
assistant."},{"role":"user","content":"How do I reset my
 password?"},{"role":"assistant","content":"You can reset your password from Account Settings >
 Security > Reset Password."}]}
7. Key concepts you should know
LLM Fine-Tuning Basics | Page 2
Base model: the original pre-trained model that you start from.
Weights: the internal numeric parameters of the model. Fine-tuning changes these weights or
adds small trainable layers.
Epoch: one full pass through the training dataset.
Learning rate: how aggressively the model updates during training. Too high can damage
behavior; too low may learn slowly.
Overfitting: when the model memorizes training examples and performs poorly on new inputs.
Validation set: a held-out set used during training to check whether the model is improving.

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
orms poorly on new inputs.
Validation set: a held-out set used during training to check whether the model is improving.
Evaluation set: a separate set used after training to judge final quality.
Catastrophic forgetting: when fine-tuning makes the model worse at general abilities it
previously had.
8. Evaluation and safety checks
Evaluation tells you whether fine-tuning actually helped.
- Compare against the base model and a strong prompted baseline.
- Use task-specific metrics where possible, such as accuracy, F1 score, exact match, JSON
 validity, or extraction precision.
- Run human review for tone, correctness, refusal behavior, and edge cases.
- Test ambiguous inputs, adversarial prompts, private data handling, and harmful requests.
- Monitor real-world usage after deployment because

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
, adversarial prompts, private data handling, and harmful requests.
- Monitor real-world usage after deployment because offline evaluation never catches
 everything.
9. Common mistakes
- Fine-tuning when a better prompt would solve the problem.
- Fine-tuning to add facts that change often instead of using RAG.
- Using low-quality or inconsistent training data.
- Training for too many epochs and overfitting.
- Testing only easy examples and ignoring failure cases.
- Forgetting privacy, security, and data licensing checks.
10. When should you fine-tune?
Fine-tuning is a good option when you have a repeated task with clear expected outputs,
prompting alone is too inconsistent, you have enough high-quality examples, and you can
evaluate whether the model improved.
Fine-tuning is probably not

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
nt, you have enough high-quality examples, and you can
evaluate whether the model improved.
Fine-tuning is probably not the best first choice when the problem can be solved with a
short prompt, the model needs access to documents or updated facts, you do not have clean
training data, or you cannot test the model properly.
11. Beginner roadmap
1. Understand prompting, tokens, context windows, embeddings, and RAG basics.
2. Create a small instruction-response dataset for one narrow task.
3. Build a baseline using prompting only and save example outputs.
4. Fine-tune a small model or use an API fine-tuning service.
5. Evaluate outputs on unseen examples and compare against the baseline.
LLM Fine-Tuning Basics | Page 3
6. Improve the dataset based on failures instead of blindly increasing

title: llm_fine_tuning_basics.pdf
content:
st the baseline.
LLM Fine-Tuning Basics | Page 3
6. Improve the dataset based on failures instead of blindly increasing training time.
Final takeaway
KEY: Fine-tuning is not magic. It is a controlled way to teach a model repeatable behavior
 using carefully selected examples. The quality of the dataset and evaluation process
 matters more than the amount of training.
For most projects, the best order is: start with prompting, add RAG if the model needs
external knowledge, and fine-tune only when you need learned, repeatable behavior.
LLM Fine-Tuning Basics | Page 4
